summary: This text outlines Socrates' foundational contributions to philosophy, specifically his theories on the soul, ethics, and epistemology. It highlights his dialectical method of inquiry, his belief that virtue is synonymous with knowledge, and his core argument that all wrongdoing is involuntary and stems from ignorance.

principles.statement: The unexamined life is not worth living.
principles.rationale: The best way to live is to actively examine one's life through reasoning and dialectical inquiry in order to seek wisdom and care for the soul.
principles.source_quote: The unexamined life is not worth living! The best manner to examinee that life is through reasoning which employs the dialectical method of inquiry.

principles.statement: No one does wrong voluntarily; evil is the result of ignorance.
principles.rationale: People always choose what they perceive to be good or beneficial for themselves. When they commit an evil act, it is because they have mistakenly identified it as a good.
principles.source_quote: Socrates believed that no one does wrong voluntarily. Evil is the result of ignorance. If people knew what was the right thing to do they would do it.

principles.rationale: Truth, beauty, and knowledge are not separate, specialized actions, but are all united together in the concept of the GOOD.
principles.source_quote: Further, Socrates held that all virtue is one! Virtue is GOOD. Truth is GOOD. Beauty is GOOD. Knowledge is GOOD. The true, good and beautiful are all GOOD and united in the GOOD as ONE.

frameworks.name: The Dialectical Process (Socratic Method)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When seeking to gain knowledge, establish the truth of a hypothesis, and arrive at universal definitions.
steps: Challenge initial hypotheses and examine them for presumptions and assumptions.
Deduce the consequences of the hypothesis to test it against facts.
Look for a coherent and consistent set of ideas; a system of thought.
frameworks.source_quote: Socrates would examine theories (logoi) using the dialectic method, which was similar to a conversational pattern with many questions. Socrates would challenge initial hypotheses and examine them for presumptions and assumptions.

mental_models.name: Midwife to ideas
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the dialectical method. Instead of simply implanting information into someone's mind, the philosopher asks questions to help the person 'give birth' to the knowledge, truth, and virtue that already resides within them.

heuristics: Always choose the best of the options available.
If someone does wrong, educate them to see their error rather than assuming malice.
Test theories by deducing their consequences and looking to facts.

signature_quotes.text: The unexamined life is not worth living!
signature_quotes.context: Socrates' core belief regarding the necessity of reasoning and inquiry.

signature_quotes.text: Evil is the result of ignorance.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why people commit wrong acts.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that people do wrong voluntarily while knowing it is truly bad for them.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: People always choose what they think will benefit them. Wrongdoers simply mistake an evil act for a good one; therefore, wrongdoing is involuntary and stems from ignorance.

anti_patterns.description: Using questioning merely to win debates and gain power (like the Sophists).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Questioning should be used to pursue truth and universal virtue, not to train in specialized techniques for personal gain.
